messages.content: Hi there ! Give me the reference of a modern 17" or 19" screen with a native resolution of 640x480

I'm sorry, but it is highly unlikely to find a modern 17" or 19" screen with a native resolution of 640x480 as that resolution has been obsolete for many years. Most modern screens come with much higher native resolutions, with 1080p (1920x1080) being the minimum standard. However, you may be able to find a used or refurbished screen with that resolution on online marketplaces such as eBay or Amazon.
Then give me a reference of any flat panel with a native resolution of 640x480

One example of a flat panel display with a native resolution of 640x480 is the Koolertron 8 inch TFT LCD Monitor with VGA/AV/HDMI Input. This is a smaller screen than you were asking for, but at that resolution, it is difficult to find larger screens that are currently in production. It is designed for use with small devices such as Raspberry Pi or security cameras. You can find it on sites like Amazon or AliExpress.
Give me the reference of an old 17" or 19" screen with a native resolution of 640x480, it can be from any time

One example of an old 17" screen with a native resolution of 640x480 is the HP D8902A 17-inch CRT Monitor. This CRT monitor was produced in the early 2000s and has a VGA input. Another example is the NEC MultiSync FE700 17" CRT Monitor. This monitor was also produced in the early 2000s and has a VGA input. As for a 19" CRT monitor with a native resolution of 640x480, there were not many models made since a 19" screen was typically intended for higher resolutions. However, you may be able to find some models from brands like NEC or ViewSonic with that resolution on online marketplaces or vintage electronics stores.
Give me the reference of an old 17" or 19" LCD screen with a native resolution of 640x480, it can be from any time

LCD displays with a native resolution of 640x480 were not very common, especially in 17" and 19" sizes. However, one example of an old 17" screen with a native resolution of 640x480 is the Sharp LL-T17A3 17-inch LCD Monitor. This monitor was released in the early 2000s, has a VGA input, and a 4:3 aspect ratio. Another example is the Hyundai ImageQuest Q770 17" LCD Monitor which was also produced in the early 2000s and has a VGA input. It's worth noting that finding an old 19" LCD monitor with a native resolution of 640x480 may be difficult, as this resolution was typically only found on smaller screens.
Give me the reference of an old 17" or 19" IPS screen with a native resolution of 640x480, it can be from any time

As far as I know, it is not possible to find 17" or 19" IPS screens with a native resolution of 640x480 as IPS technology was not widely used in screens with such a low resolution. IPS screens became popular in the late 2000s and offered higher resolutions and better color reproduction than older display technologies. However, you may be able to find some old 17" or 19" screens with a low resolution and IPS technology if you search for vintage or refurbished screens from the early 2000s. It is worth noting that the picture quality of these screens may not be as good as modern displays due to their low resolution and age.
Give me the reference of an old 19" LCD screen with a native resolution of 640x480, it can be from any time

As I mentioned earlier, it is highly unlikely to find an old 19" LCD screen with a native resolution of 640x480, as this resolution was typically only available on smaller screens. However, one example of an old 19" LCD screen with a low resolution is the Dell E193FP 19" LCD Monitor. This monitor was released in the mid-2000s and has a native resolution of 1280x1024, but it can display 640x480 scaled to fit the screen. Another possible option is the IBM 6657-HG0 19" LCD Monitor, which was also produced in the mid-2000s and has a native resolution of 1280x1024, but can display lower resolutions scaled to fit the screen. Please note that these monitors are old and may not meet modern standards for picture quality or connectivity options.
What is the best display to use with a system exclusively outputing a resolution of 640x480 ? it can be from any time or have any technology

If you are exclusively outputting a resolution of 640x480, it would be best to use a CRT monitor as the image quality would be sharp and clear. CRT monitors have the advantage of being able to display a wide range of resolutions without any scaling or distortion. You can find old CRT monitors with a 4:3 aspect ratio and a native resolution of 640x480 that were commonly used in the 1990s and early 2000s. One good example of a 17" CRT monitor with a native resolution of 640x480 is the Sony CPD-G400, which was released in the late 1990s. Another option is the ViewSonic E70fB, which is a 17" CRT monitor that was popular in the early 2000s and has a native resolution of 1280x960, but it can display 640x480 scaled to fit the screen without any distortion.
Same question, but exlusively with flat panels

If you are exclusively outputting a resolution of 640x480 and would like to use a flat panel display, then it would be best to choose a display that has a native resolution close to 640x480 to avoid scaling and distortion. One good option could be a 4:3 aspect ratio 15-inch or 17-inch LCD screen with a native resolution of 1024x768. Such displays were common in the 2000s and early 2010s and can display 640x480 without any scaling or distortion. 

One example of a 15-inch LCD display with a native resolution of 1024x768 is the Dell E151FPb, which was released in the early 2000s and has both VGA and DVI inputs. Another option is the Acer AL1716 17" LCD Monitor, which was released in the mid-2000s and also has a native resolution of 1280x1024 but can display lower resolutions scaled to fit the screen.

Please note that while using a flat panel display with a non-native resolution can result in distortion or blurrier images, it can still be functional for general computing tasks.
How is 1024x768 monitor a better choice to use for the targetted resolution, against other resolution monitors such as 1280x960 ?

While a monitor with a native resolution of 1280x960 can display a resolution of 640x480, the display may become distorted or appear pixelated, as the resolution is not evenly scaled. A monitor with a native resolution of 1024x768, on the other hand, has a 4:3 aspect ratio, which is the same aspect ratio as 640x480. This means that the image will be displayed without distortion or stretching, making it a better choice for displaying a resolution of 640x480. Since a monitor with a native resolution of 1024x768 has a higher maximum resolution than 640x480, the computer's operating system can still display higher resolution images, videos, or graphics when needed, while still allowing 640x480 to be displayed without scaling or distortion.
However, 1280x960 is exactly two times 640x480 and is also 4:3, wouldn't that be better ?

Yes, you are correct. A monitor with a native resolution of 1280x960 would be an equally good choice as it also has an aspect ratio of 4:3. This resolution is exactly 2 times 640x480, so it can easily display 640x480 by scaling it evenly without any distortion or stretching. In fact, the resolution of 1280x960 provides a higher pixel density compared to a resolution of 1024x768. This means that images, text, and graphics will appear sharper and more detailed on a 1280x960 monitor than on a 1024x768 monitor. So, if you have an option to choose between a 1024x768 monitor and a 1280x960 monitor, both with native 4:3 aspect ratio, you can choose the 1280x960 one for a better overall display quality.
How about a monitor with a resolution three times or four times higher than 640x480 ? Is it better or worse than a monitor with two times the resolution of 640x480 ?

A monitor with a resolution that is three or four times higher than 640x480 may not be the best choice for exclusively displaying content at 640x480 resolution. If a higher resolution monitor is used, the 640x480 resolution will need to be scaled up or interpolated to fit the higher resolution screen. This can cause the image to appear blurry or pixelated since the higher resolution monitor is stretching or "repeating" the pixels to fit the larger screen size. In this case, a monitor with a native resolution of 1280x960 would be a better choice since it's an exact 2x resolution of 640x480, which means that each pixel in the lower resolution image can be displayed as a block of four pixels in the 1280x960 display.

However, if you occasionally use higher resolutions for other purposes, such as image editing or video production, then a monitor with a higher resolution may be a better overall choice. This is because higher resolution displays can display more detailed and clearer images, and also offer more screen real estate for multitasking.
How about a monitor with a resolution of 2560x1920 ? This is also a 4:3 aspect ratio, but is exactly three times 640x480, will it be like 1280x960 but better since it is two times that resolution ?

The resolution of 2560x1920 is not a 4:3 aspect ratio, but a 5:4 aspect ratio. However, if we consider a 4:3 area of this display, it would have a native resolution of 2048x1536. This means that displaying content at a resolution of 640x480 would require significant scaling to fit the larger monitor, which could result in a blurry or pixelated image. While the display may appear sharper and more detailed due to the higher resolution, it still may not be the best choice for exclusively displaying content at 640x480 resolution without distortion or scaling.

In general, when working with lower-resolution content such as 640x480, it's best to choose a monitor with a native resolution that is evenly scaled, such as a resolution that is an exact multiple of the target resolution (for example, a 2x or 3x multiple). The display will look much sharper, without any scaling artifacts or image distortion. However, if you need a monitor for other purposes, like photo or video editing, a higher resolution monitor may be an excellent choice.
Is 2x better or 3x better ?

It's hard to say whether 2x or 3x is definitively better, as it depends on your specific needs and preferences. However, there are a few things to consider that may help you decide.

A monitor with a native resolution that is 2x higher than the target resolution (640x480) will display each pixel of the target resolution as a 2x2 block of pixels, without any intermediary pixels. This means that the image will appear sharp and crisp, and no scaling artifacts or distortion will be introduced. Examples of such monitors include those with a native resolution of 1280x960 or 1280x1024.

On the other hand, if you choose a monitor with a native resolution that is 3x higher than the target resolution, each pixel of the target resolution will be displayed as a 3x3 block of pixels. This may result in slightly lower image quality, since many more pixels are being added to the original image. However, if you need the extra screen real estate for multitasking or other purposes, a monitor with a higher native resolution may still be a good choice.

Ultimately, the decision between 2x and 3x is up to personal preference and what you plan to use the monitor for. If you prioritize image quality, go for a monitor with a 2x native resolution. If you prefer more screen space or need the higher resolution for other tasks, a 3x monitor may be a better choice.
